title: Disagreement, information quality and asset prices
abstract: We present an analytical solution for a pure exchange economy featuring a continuum of agents with disagreement, time-varying information quality, and reference-dependent preferences. Our general equilibrium model exhibits stationary dynamics. By examining the implications of the model, we find that the commonly studied asset pricing channels of disagreement have limited quantitative significance. On the other hand, variations in information quality, which affect disagreement levels, lead to substantial excess stock price volatility. This finding contributes significantly to explaining the equity premium and sheds light on empirical relationships between forecast dispersion and asset prices, the upward sloping real yield curve, and long-term yield movements.

author.Costas Xiouros*: Department of Finance, BI Norwegian Business School, Oslo, N-0442, Norway
author.Fernando Zapatero: Finance Department, Questrom School of Business, Boston University, Boston, 02215, MA, United States of America

article.1. Introduction: Participants in financial markets continuously receive vast amounts of complex information, often conflicting in nature. The quality of this information varies over time as economic and market conditions change. In general, market participants interpret information differently, especially when the quality of information is low. The distortionary effects of disagreement are far-reaching, both for the real and the financial economy.1 In this paper, we investigate the effects of disagreement and changes in information quality on asset prices in an endowment economy. We look at both stock and bond market implications. To reach our goal, we go beyond a qualitative analysis and quantify various effects, with a model that is estimated using data from professional forecasters and that explains several asset pricing facts.We study the asset pricing effects of disagreement and information quality with a dynamic model of the cross-section of beliefs about the growth rate of the economy. There is a continuum of agents that update their beliefs constantly and yet agree to disagree, in line with the evidence provided by Kandel and Pearson (1995). As in Veronesi (2000), the mean growth rate of the economy varies over time and agents receive a common signal regarding these changes, with a certain level of information quality.2 In addition, we consider disagreement among agents, resulting from a set of “private” noisy signals, similar to Scheinkman and Xiong (2003). Agents believe that “their” signals have information whose perceived informativeness, given a rationality constraint, is inversely proportional to information quality. This captures the conventional wisdom that when common information is weak or absent, individuals revert to their “tribal” beliefs. Consequently, when the information quality of the common signal deteriorates, disagreement increases and leads to heterogeneity in expectations, that we refer to as forecast dispersion. Given the processes we derive for the mean and the dispersion in forecasts, we estimate the disagreement model parameters using data from the Survey of Professional Forecasters on GDP growth forecasts.Regarding preferences, we assume that agents have reference-dependent utility for several reasons. Firstly, many experimental studies by the psychologists and economists responsible for the inception and growth of behavioral economics, conclude that an essential feature in economic and financial decision-making is the existence and critical role of a reference value—for example, current wealth, wealth of peers, a benchmark, are some of the most frequently used in the literature, among many others–for the decision-maker.3 Secondly, we adopt the specification proposed by Constantinides (1990) regarding the role of the reference point, as it has significant impact on how agents assess and price risk. To accurately quantify the effects of disagreement and information quality, we require our model to explain the market price of risk, which disagreement alone cannot accomplish, as shown by Panageas (2020). Thirdly, the absence of a reference point would imply unrealistically high levels of individual consumption risk and bankruptcy rates, considering the level of disagreement observed in reality.By acknowledging the influence of a reference point, we introduce a more realistic and nuanced understanding of decision-making under uncertainty. Nevertheless, despite the added complexity, we clearly distinguish between the effects of disagreement and those of reference-dependent preferences as these two factors operate independently of each other. Furthermore, we also incorporate the implications for standard constant relative risk aversion preferences, as they represent a special case of the preferences we adopt.Among the different possible ways to incorporate reference-dependence in preferences, we adapt the model of habit-formation of Campbell and Cochrane (1999) to non-i.i.d. consumption growth. This model has been very influential in the mainstream asset pricing literature, as well as in the behavioral economics camp–for example, Rayo and Becker (2007). An important feature of this model is that the persistence in the habit generates variation in the price of risk, which explains the stock return predictability and accounts for a significant portion of the stock return volatility.Despite the rich model, we derive the state dynamics, the risk-free rate, and the market price of risk in closed form. Specifically, we derive how the distribution of types evolves over time as a result of the updating of beliefs and investing performance.4 Speculation, which refers to the fact that agents choose their exposures to the aggregate shock depending on their expectations, creates differences in the investing performance and, thus, changes in the wealth distribution.5 The distribution of types is characterized by a weighted mean and a weighted standard deviation of beliefs, where the weights are related to consumption and wealth.6 We refer to these moments as market expectation and market forecast dispersion, respectively. An important characteristic of the equilibrium is that it is stationary. In contrast, most models of heterogeneous beliefs consider only a finite set of types which yield non-stationary equilibria because, as Yan (2008) shows, only one type survives at the end.Our results and quantitative analysis allow us to clearly disentangle and quantify the effects of all three elements: disagreement, time-varying information quality, and habit-formation preferences. For example, we derive the representative agent formulation of the equilibrium, which provides equivalent asset pricing effects. Our analysis highlights which effects are quantitatively important. In addition, the model explains several asset pricing facts, like the equity premium, the stock market volatility, the low correlation between the risk-free rate and the stock market, the high correlation between the stock market and the forecast dispersion, bond risk premia, and the upward sloping yield curve.Our first result is that if disagreement and information quality are constant, disagreement has negligible effect on stock price variations. This stands in contrast to most of the existing literature, which finds that disagreement alone generates excess stock price volatility.7 The literature predicts excess volatility through two channels. The first channel is the effect that disagreement has on the variation of the market expectation, or often referred to as sentiment. Disagreement affects the fluctuations in the market expectation first due to changes in the wealth distribution caused by speculation. Specifically, growth rate realizations benefit those agents whose expectations were more correct in hindsight. Thus, since speculation increases the sensitivity of the market expectation to growth rate shocks, these excess fluctuations can potentially increase both asset price volatilities and risk premia. This is the focus, for example, of Atmaz and Basak (2018). However, the market expectation also fluctuates due to the updating of beliefs. Because of this, the market expectation inherits the persistency of the true mean growth rate. This is the same as the persistency of the mean forecast in the data, which is not high enough to generate significant stock price fluctuations. In addition, higher disagreement in our model is intimately connected to lower information quality and, lower information quality means that agents learn less about the shocks to the economy from available information. Consequently, higher disagreement typically makes the market expectation less, rather than more volatile, if it is connected with lower information quality.The second channel through which disagreement may generate excess volatility is fluctuations in the market forecast dispersion. However, most studies, e.g., Zapatero (1998), Buraschi and Jiltsov (2006), David (2008) and Dumas et al. (2009), generate this prediction because they analyze models with only two agents. In this case, the shocks to the wealth distribution caused by speculation introduce permanent shocks to the market forecast dispersion. In contrast, with a continuum of types, we show that the market forecast dispersion is constant when disagreement and information quality do not change.8 Essentially, at the steady state the decrease in dispersion caused by speculation is perfectly offset by the increase caused by disagreement.9 This result highlights the importance of taking into consideration the law of large numbers (LLN) when studying the effects of agent heterogeneity.Therefore, dynamic effects of disagreement depend on the existence of shocks to information quality. In fact, our second result is that, with disagreement, variations in information quality create significant excess stock price variations. In our calibrated model, this excess volatility accounts for almost half of the volatility we observe in the data. Due to the inverse relation between disagreement and information quality, shocks to information quality generate changes in the market forecast dispersion. The resulting speculative positions, in turn, affect the economy's risk-free rate because beliefs have asymmetric effects on state prices, as shown by Jouini and Napp, 2006a, Jouini and Napp, 2006b, Jouini and Napp, 2007. Therefore, persistent shocks to information quality translate to persistent shocks to the risk-free rate, that are sizeable enough to cause large stock price fluctuations. In our calibration, the risk-free rate is affected positively by the market forecast dispersion, because relative pessimists have stronger effect on the aggregate demand for savings. Hence, the stock market reacts positively to positive shocks to information quality, since these drive down disagreement, the market forecast dispersion and, hence, the risk-free rate. Through this mechanism, the model also explains the fact that forecast dispersion in the data is strongly negatively correlated with the stock market.Our third result is that, without disagreement, variations in information quality also have negligible effects on both the stock price volatility and the equity premium. The asset pricing effects of information quality with a representative agent are studied, for example, by Veronesi (2000) in an endowment economy and by Ai (2010) in a production economy.10 A common underlying mechanism is that, in the presence of an informative independent signal–the common signal in our model–learning from the growth rate realizations is less relevant. Consequently, higher information quality makes the market expectation less sensitive to growth rate realizations, which affects both the equity premium and the stock return volatility. However, as we noted above, our quantitative analysis shows that the fluctuations in the market expectation are not persistent enough to have other than negligible effects on stock prices. Therefore, any modifications in the dynamics of the market expectation due to changes in information quality are also negligible for stock prices.From the above results it becomes clear that it is the variations in disagreement, which in our model are produced by variations in information quality, that generate quantitatively important effects for asset prices; while the effects of either constant disagreement or stochastic information quality in isolation, as studied in the previous literature, are not quantitatively important. This leads us to our fourth result, which is that the excess stock market volatility generated by the interaction of these two features, gives rise to a term premium that increases the equity premium. The reason is first, that the disagreement among forecasters is counter-cyclical in the data, which is explained by information quality being positively affected and strongly dependent on growth rate shocks. Secondly, as explained earlier, the stock market reacts positively to increases in information quality. As a result, investors require compensation for the fact that the stock market is affected negatively by adverse aggregate shocks, due to the decrease in information quality and the increase in disagreement.The term premium that emerges in our model also has substantial implications for the bond market. A well-established fact regarding bond prices is the upward sloping nominal yield curve. The existing literature often attributes this phenomenon to an inflation risk premium, as demonstrated by Piazzesi and Schneider (2006), Rudebusch and Swanson (2012), Bansal and Shaliastovich (2013), and Kung (2015). However, these models also predict a downward sloping real yield curve, which contradicts the empirical evidence provided by Roll (2004) and Hsu et al. (2021). The negative slope in the real yield curve results from positive autocorrelation in aggregate consumption growth, as shown by Campbell (1986). Wachter (2006) and Hsu et al. (2021) propose that the upward sloping real yield curve can be explained by versions of habit formation preferences, where the intertemporal substitution motive reacts more strongly to the precautionary savings motive.We adopt the approach of Campbell and Cochrane (1999), assuming that the variations in these two motives due to habit cancel each other out. As a result, in the absence of disagreement or time-varying information quality, the real yield curve has a downward slope. Incorporating disagreement and variations in information quality naturally leads to the positive slope observed in the real yield curve based on Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities (TIPS) data. Moreover, the real bond risk premia and the long-term yield volatilities of our model are comparable to those observed in the data.Given the pivotal role that market forecast dispersion plays in determining long-term real yields, we investigate whether this is supported by evidence. Using the forecast dispersion among forecasters as a proxy, we make the following observations: Firstly, the forecast dispersion exhibits correlations with future short-term rates that closely align with the correlations predicted by the model. Secondly, the model's implied long-term real yields demonstrate significant correlations with the real yields inferred from the data. For instance, the correlation for the 20-year yield is 0.45, indicating a substantial improvement compared to the correlations of −0.29 and −0.37 obtained when assuming constant information quality or no disagreement, respectively.To solve the equilibrium in closed form, we assume that markets are complete. This assumption allows agents to trade securities that depend on their “private” signals, which may initially appear extreme. However, it becomes reasonable when considering that these signals pertain to the overall economy and should therefore have relevance to traded assets such as stocks, bonds, or derivatives.Nevertheless, to further strengthen our theory, we also explore an incomplete market scenario where agents are unable to trade securities that depend on their signals. Leveraging the LLN, we characterize the equilibrium in a manner comparable to the complete market case. The equilibrium adjusts based on the extent to which each individual's optimal consumption-to-wealth ratio depends on their expectations. Consequently, in the case of agents with log-utility preferences, where the consumption-to-wealth ratio is constant, the equilibrium is equivalent to the complete market case. For the more general case of power utility preferences with habit, through quantitative analysis and approximation, we illustrate that this incompleteness has minimal consequences. The underlying intuition is straightforward: the signals introduce a minor idiosyncratic risk to individual consumption, resulting in insignificant aggregate effects.Our paper is mostly related to the long literature on heterogeneous beliefs.11 Miller (1977) hypothesizes that heterogeneous expectations with short sale constraints lead to overvaluation, because prices overweight the valuation of optimistic investors. Harrison and Kreps (1978) and Scheinkman and Xiong (2003) substantiate this hypothesis with partial equilibrium analyses, assuming, however, risk-neutral agents. Gallmeyer and Hollifield (2008) extend the analysis to general equilibrium with risk-averse agents and find that heterogeneous expectations with short-sale constraints may increase or reduce the stock price depending on the investors' intertemporal elasticity of substitution.12 Similarly, Jouini and Napp (2006a) and Jouini and Napp (2006b) show that in a frictionless setting heterogeneity in beliefs may increase or decrease stock prices because, depending on the risk preference parameters, it may decrease or increase the risk-free interest rate.13Detemple and Murthy (1994) in a production economy and Zapatero (1998) in a pure exchange economy, analyze the asset pricing effects of heterogeneous beliefs in general equilibrium. Basak (2000) shows that heterogeneous beliefs over non-fundamentals give rise to additional priced factors and Basak (2005) expresses the equilibrium in the form of a representative agent and derives the price of risk in the case of CRRA preferences. Buraschi and Jiltsov (2006) study the effects on options and draw a link between disagreement, option volumes and option prices.14 Dumas et al. (2009) study the “sentiment” risk caused by an overconfident investor, which generates excess volatility in stock prices, and analyze the optimal behavior of the rational investor, who knows that the overconfident investor is wrong, but might get lucky. Xiong and Yan (2010) and Ehling et al. (2018) find that heterogeneity in inflation expectations generates excess volatility in bond yields.Methodologically, our study is related to Xiouros and Zapatero (2010), Cvitanić and Malamud (2011), and Atmaz and Basak (2018) who utilize an appropriate distributional assumption over a continuum of types to provide analytical results of the market equilibrium. Cvitanić et al. (2011) and Bhamra and Uppal (2014) offer general equilibrium analyses of economies with both preference and belief heterogeneity, and a number of analytical results in terms of asset pricing quantities, as well as results pertaining to agent survival and the long-run properties of those economies.Our study is distinct from previous literature in that it incorporates several elements that have not been combined in a single study before. Specifically, we examine a continuum of agents over an infinite horizon, updating beliefs regarding the latent underlying state of the economy, and reference-dependent preferences. Additionally, we introduce a novel component to this literature: information quality, which determines the extent of disagreement among agents. Notably, our model generates a stationary equilibrium, and we conduct a quantitative analysis to examine the various effects.As previously stated, our study is also related to the literature on the term-structure of interest rates and the literature regarding the effects of information quality on asset prices. Regarding the term-structure literature, our main contribution is to provide an alternative explanation of the term premium, supported by empirical evidence. In regard to information quality, our study challenges whether variations in information quality alone have a significant impact on asset prices.Finally, our study also intersects with the literature that quantitatively explains the time-series characteristics of asset prices, including prominent features such as the equity premium, volatility of valuation ratios, and properties of the risk-free rate. Noteworthy examples of this literature include the habit-formation models proposed by Campbell and Cochrane (1999) and Wachter (2006), the long-run risk model by Bansal and Yaron (2004), and the rare disaster models by Barro (2006) and Gabaix (2012).In our contribution to this field, we extend the existing literature by incorporating heterogeneous beliefs into a habit-formation model. This enriched model not only accounts for the equity premium and stock return volatility but also explains various relationships between forecast dispersion and asset prices, as well as relevant bond market facts. Notably, we introduce a novel factor, namely information quality in the context of disagreement, which we find to have significant implications for asset prices.
article.2. A model of disagreement: We consider an endowment economy, where agents face aggregate consumption risk. Agents, however, differ in their expectations regarding the growth rate of the economy, because they disagree on their interpretation of common information. All information is available to all agents so that there is no need to learn from asset prices. The model has three distinctive features: a continuum of agents, a common informative signal, and the fluctuating degree to which the agents interpret information differently, which we call disagreement. Variations in disagreement are caused by shocks to information quality. These features generate rich dynamics of the distribution of beliefs that allow us to estimate the model using data from the Survey of Professional Forecasters (SPF) provided by the Philadelphia Fed.2.1. Data and estimationTo estimate the model we use aggregate consumption data and data from the Survey of Professional Forecasters (SPF). We use the annual consumption data provided by the National Income and Product Accounts (NIPA) from 1929 to 2017 and compute the per-capita consumption of non-durable goods and services. We adjust the annual growth rates for inflation by subtracting the growth in the annual average CPI index–data taken from the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS). We simulate the model at quarterly frequency and time-aggregate to compute annual moments.In the data, an obvious candidate for forecast dispersion ν¯, used in the literature, e.g. by David (2008) and Ehling et al. (2018), is the cross-sectional dispersion in macroeconomic forecasts. From the SPF, we use the real GDP forecasts for the current quarter and for the quarter ahead. The data is quarterly and spans the period from 1968Q3 to 2016Q4. Every quarter there are between 9 and 83 professional forecasts with an average of 38.5 and standard deviation of 12. From the level data we compute growth rates which then we adjust for population growth (computed from NIPA tables) to obtain real quarterly per-capita growth forecasts. Then, for every quarter we compute the cross-sectional mean forecast (μ¯) and the dispersion in forecasts (ν¯). To lessen the effect of outliers we compute the dispersion as the distance between two specific percentiles: those of a normal distribution half a standard deviation left and right from the mean. The dispersion measure corresponds, thus, to the cross-sectional standard deviation in the case the distribution of forecasts is Gaussian. We also collect quarterly data for real per-capita GDP growth over the same period as the SPF data. We thus analyze the moments of the average forecast and the dispersion in forecasts in relation to the variable in concern.To compute model moments we run 1,000 simulations with 400 periods each and a burn-in of 100 periods. The model is estimated using the Simulated Method of Moments, trying to match 16 moments with 10 parameters. Table 1 shows the moments for the data alongside those of the estimated model. The parenthesis next to each data statistic is the standard error estimated using Newey and West (1987) and 16 lags. The parenthesis next to each model moment shows the t-statistic of the hypothesis that the data moment is equal to the model value. From the table we see that the differences between the model and the data are statistically insignificant, with the highest t-statistic being 1.28 and the rest less than 1. article.3. Investor preferences and market equil: The disagreement model is analyzed and estimated irrespective of investor preferences, wealth distribution and market structure, elements that are necessary to fully characterize the economy and generate asset pricing predictions. We assume that markets are complete so that agents can choose their optimal consumption paths through trading.17 We analyze a case with incomplete markets in Section 7, where agents cannot trade on contingent claims that depend on their signals.3.1. Risk preferencesOur choice of utility function for the individuals that populate the economy in our model is based on several considerations. First, the standard utility functions used in dynamic asset pricing models (namely CRRA utility) have failed at explaining many of the properties of asset prices, especially –but not exclusively—the market price of risk. Second, one of the main takeaways of the by now vast literature on behavioral economics is the critical importance of a reference point in financial decision-making –this is a key element in the Prospect Theory of Kahneman and Tversky (1979) and many of the subsequent studies, like Kőszegi and Rabin, 2006, Kőszegi and Rabin, 2009. Third, the absence of a reference point would imply unrealistically high consumption risk and bankruptcy probability for an individual, given the level of disagreement we observe.There are many ways to incorporate the reference point in the utility function. Here we select the “difference” habit formation utility postulated by Sundaresan (1989) and Constantinides (1990), culminating in the influential work of Campbell and Cochrane (1999). Rayo and Becker (2007) provide an evolutionary explanation of the prevalence of habit preferences. In particular, we assume that all agents live forever and exhibit identical power utility preferences with external habit:(11)Eti∑τ=0∞βτ(Ct+τi−Ht+τ)1−γ−11−γ,∀i∈[0,1], where Ci denotes the consumption of agent i and H the common external habit –the reference point. The subjective discount factor is β<1 and γ>0 is the utility curvature parameter. When γ=1 the preferences take the logarithmic form. The habit permits to explain the high market Sharpe ratio. Specifically, the Arrow-Pratt measure of relative risk aversion in a certain period t is given by γCti/(Cti−Ht). Thus, lower consumption or higher level of habit implies higher risk aversion.When Ht=0, agents exhibit the standard CRRA preferences. To clarify the effects of disagreement and separate them from those coming from the habit preferences, we derive the equilibrium without specifying how the habit is formed. This implies that the equilibrium characterization embeds the case with CRRA preferences.3.2. The market equilibriumAgents maximize their utility (11) every period subject to their period budget constraint. The period budget constraints can be replaced by the intertemporal budget constraint according to which the present value of an agent's consumption stream should be less or equal to the initial total wealth, which equals the present value of the income stream plus the initial financial wealth. The present value of a stream of cash-flows can be written using the state price process p, where p0(zt) gives the price at time 0 of a unit of consumption in state zt. Thus, the intertemporal budget constraint is written as(12)∑t=0∞∫Ctip0(zt)dzt⩽W0i, where W0i is the initial total wealth of agent i.The economy is in equilibrium when agents choose their consumption processes optimally and the financial and consumption markets clear. Due to completeness, financial markets clear if and only if the consumption good market clears in all states. The market clearing condition in a given period t is then given by setting the integral of the consumption across agents equal to the per-capita consumption Ct. Equivalently, we can set the integral of the consumption surplus shares across agents equal to one,(13)Ct=∫01Ctidi⇔1=∫01αtidi, where αti:=Cti−HtCt−Ht.Note that the consumption surplus (share) distribution across agents, (αi,i∈[0,1]), is a continuous probability density function. Based on this distribution we define another distribution which, as we will show later, characterizes asset prices; namely, the consumption surplus share distribution across types (beliefs), which we denote with αt(μ). It is defined similarly to nt(μ) –the distribution of beliefs defined in section 2.2– but instead of equal weights we use consumption surplus shares.18 The mean and variance of this distribution are given by,(14)μ˜t:=∫01αtiμtidiandν˜t2:=∫01αti(μti−μt)2di. We refer to μ˜ as the market expectation and ν˜ as the market forecast dispersion, which are endogenously determined in equilibrium. We use μ˜ to specify the process of χ in (10). Next, we characterize the equilibrium allocations and state prices with the following proposition. To see clearly the effect of disagreement, let(15)Nt,t+1:=β⋅(Ct+1−Ht+1Ct−Ht)−γ, which is the stochastic discount factor between period t and t+1 in the homogeneous agent economy.Proposition 2In equilibrium, the consumption surplus shares evolve according toαt+1i=αtiϕ(gt+1;μti,σt2)1/γ∫01αtjϕ(gt+1;μtj,σt2)1/γdj,i∈[0,1], where ϕ(x;μ,σ2) denotes the normal density with mean μ and standard deviation σ. The price of state zt+1 in period t is given bypt(zt+1)=Nt+1⋅f(zt+1|Ft,gt+1)⋅[∫01αtiϕ(gt+1;μti,σt2)1/γdi]γ, where σt is defined in Lemma 1 and f(zt+1|Ft,gt+1) denotes the density of state zt+1 conditional on the information at time t and the realization gt+1.Agents, in maximizing their utility, attempt to smooth their consumption surplus shares both across states and across time. In the absence of belief heterogeneity there is perfect risk-sharing, in which case the consumption surplus shares are constant. However, disagreement is incompatible with perfect risk sharing because this would imply disagreement about state prices and, hence, about prices of financial securities. Since agents agree on prices in equilibrium, they deviate from perfect risk sharing and their consumption surplus shares vary over time. Specifically, an agent's consumption surplus share increases in states that the agent considers relatively more likely compared to the rest of the agents, and decreases otherwise.The final element we need to determine allocations and prices in equilibrium is the initial wealth distribution across agents. To provide analytical results, we use the approach in Xiouros and Zapatero (2010), also employed by Cvitanić and Malamud (2011) in the context of heterogeneous beliefs, whereby we make a convenient assumption about the initial endogenous consumption distribution. In particular, the equilibrium consumption processes and state prices admit closed form expressions if the initial consumption surplus share distribution across beliefs is Gaussian. We have already assumed that the initial distribution across beliefs, n0(μ) is Gaussian with mean μ¯0 and variance ν¯02. We thus assume that the initial wealth distribution is such that α0(μ) is Gaussian with mean μ˜0 and variance ν˜02. This implies that relatively pessimistic agents need to have a bit more wealth than average to (partially) protect themselves from the expensive states following negative shocks. Alternatively, we can assume that agents begin with a common prior, i.e. ν¯=0, in which case we need not impose any restriction on the initial wealth allocation.Proposition 3In equilibrium, the consumption surplus share distribution across beliefs αt(μ) is Gaussian at all times t⩾0 and the law of motion of the mean and variance are given byμ˜t+1=(1−φμ)μc+φμκt1+ξtμ˜t+φμ(1−κt1+ξt)gt+1+σμAμ,t+1st+1m,ν˜t+12=φμ2κt21+ξtν˜t2+σμ2Aν,t+1, whereξt:=ν˜t2γσt2. Furthermore, let P˜ denote the probability measure under which the distribution of gt+1 conditional on Ft is Gaussian with mean μ˜t and variance σ˜t2:=σt2(1+ξt). Then, the one-period stochastic discount factor (SDF) under the probability measure P˜ is given byln⁡M˜(zt,gt+1)=ln⁡Nt,t+1+1−γ2ln⁡(1+ξt), where Nt,t+1 is as specified in (15).In Proposition 3, we choose to express the SDF under the probability measure P˜, which we refer to as the market beliefs, for expositional purposes.19 It is a natural way to highlight the two channels through which disagreement affects the economy. The first channel is through the beliefs under which assets are priced. In a full information economy we would have μ˜t=μt and σ˜t=σc. In a perfectly rational homogeneous agent economy we would have μ˜t=μˇt and σ˜t2=σc2+vˇt2. The second channel is through the factor ξt which affects the level of the SDF and, hence, the level of all asset prices.In Proposition 3, we derive the SDF in terms of N, the SDF in the homogeneous agent case, to make it clear that the effects of disagreement are orthogonal to those of habit. This is evident by the fact that ξ, that enters M, and the beliefs P˜ are independent of and unaffected by habit. This means that ξ and P˜ are the same with CRRA and habit preferences. Of course, disagreement and habit interact in shaping asset prices, as we will see in our quantitative analysis, but their effects are independent of each other.There is one more quantitatively important way in which habit and disagreement interact, which is in determining the consumption risk and the survival of an agent. Given the definition of αti, the consumption growth of an agent is given by,Ct+1iCti=Ct+1Ct⋅(Ht+1/Ct+1)+αt+1i[1−(Ht+1/Ct+1)](Ht/Ct)+αti[1−(Ht/Ct)]. Notice that as habit approaches the aggregate level of consumption (Ht→Ct and Ht+1→Ct+1) the individual consumption growth approaches the per-capita consumption growth. In the opposite case where habit is zero, the individual consumption growth is entirely determined by the growth rate in αi. Therefore, if the growth in αi is more volatile than the growth in C, which is the case in our calibration, the volatility of the individual consumption growth decreases with the level of habit. Even though this is not the focus of our paper, it is important to note that habit becomes an important element in explaining the individual consumption growth volatility, given the observed level of disagreement. Further, the existence of habit ensures the survival of all agents, since as αti→0, Cti→Ht. Effectively, agents speculate according to their beliefs only with their excess wealth, meaning the wealth over the amount of wealth they need to cover their habitual level of consumption.The equilibrium has further convenient and interesting properties which are: (i) the disagreement economy admits a representative agent formulation; (ii) central to both channels through which disagreement affects asset prices is the quantity ξt, which we dub speculative activity; (iii) when information quality is constant, ξ is constant, which nullifies the main effects of disagreement; and (iv) despite the fact that αti are stochastic and the distribution of αti across agents is non-stationary, the distribution of types αt(μ) and the equilibrium are stationary. We deal with these properties next.3.3. The representative agentThe equilibrium admits a representative agent representation, in the sense that there is a homogeneous or representative agent economy with certain beliefs and preferences that generates the same asset prices. An economy is equivalent in its pricing implications if it has the same SDF under the probability measure P˜.Proposition 4Consider a representative (or homogeneous) agent economy, where the representative (or each) agent has the same preferences as the agents in the heterogeneous agent economy but with a subjective discount factor given by βˆt:=β(1+ξt)1−γ2. Further, the representative (or each) agent believes that the conditional volatility of μt and the informativeness of the common signal are given byσˆμ,t:=σμAμ,t2+Av,t+Aν,tγandρˆt:=Aμ,tAμ,t2+Av,t+Aν,tγ, respectively. Then, this economy is equivalent in its pricing implications to the heterogeneous agent economy. Since ζt=η÷ρt then Aμ,t=ρt (Proposition 1) andAμ,t2+Av,t+Aν,tγ=1+(1γ−1)Aν,t⋚1ifγ⋛1.The adjustment to the subjective discount factor corresponds to the aggregation bias of Jouini and Napp, 2006b, Jouini and Napp, 2006a, Jouini and Napp, 2007 which makes the effect of the market forecast dispersion in asset prices equivalent to an adjustment to the discount factor. We refer to this as the discount factor induced by the heterogeneity in expectations. Effectively, when γ>1 “pessimists” (μti<μ˜t) that push state prices down have a stronger effect than “optimists” (μti>μ˜t) that push prices up, and the other way round for γ<1. Hence, a mean-preserving increase in the market forecast dispersion leads to lower (higher) state prices when γ>1 (γ<1) and, for this reason, βˆt<β (βˆt>β). The adjustments to the conditional volatility of μ and information quality are required so that the process of the representative agent's expectations μˆ is identical to that of μ˜. By the same token, the conditional consumption risk of the representative agent is given by σ˜ because the uncertainty of the representative agent is equal to vt2+ν˜t2÷γ.3.4. Speculation and equilibrium dynamicsDisagreement (η>0) introduces two effects, dispersion in beliefs and speculation, and ξt is a measure of both. By speculation we mean that agents deviate from perfect risk sharing in a way that is optimal according to their beliefs. As pointed out after Proposition 2, the consumption surplus (and wealth) shares become stochastic with disagreement. In the following lemma we show that ξt, apart from being a measure of the market forecast dispersion as per its definition (Proposition 3), it also captures the consumption (and wealth) reallocation caused by speculation.Lemma 2Let the speculative activity in period t be defined as the cross-sectional variance of the sensitivity of the log growth in the consumption surplus to the log per-capita consumption growth at gt+1=μ˜t. That is, speculation is defined as the cross-sectional variance of∂∂gt+1log⁡(Ct+1i−Ht+1Cti−Ht)|gt+1=μ˜t weighted by the consumption surplus shares. The speculative activity is then given by ξt.Naturally, by the definition of ξt, the speculative activity is zero when there is no disagreement, increases with the market forecast dispersion ν˜ and decreases with the utility curvature parameter γ. Further, ν˜ decreases with information quality, since ∂Aν/∂ρ<0 (see definition of Aν in Proposition 3) for the case where ζ=η/ρ. Below we note two properties of the equilibrium related to speculation.The first is that the speculative activity ξt makes μ˜ more sensitive to aggregate consumption growth shocks. As shown by the process of μ˜ in Proposition 3, the coefficient of gt+1 is given by 1−κt÷(1+ξt), which increases with ξt. When we compare this coefficient with the equivalent coefficient in the law of motion of μ¯ (Proposition 1), given by 1−κt, we infer that κtξt represents approximately the reallocation of consumption surplus shares across types20; specifically, the wealth reallocation is the wealth gain of the agents whose beliefs are more correct in hindsight. Naturally, lower uncertainty (higher κt) increases the effect of speculation.The second property is that ξ varies only due to shocks in information quality. From the process of ν˜, we see that it is independent of both g and sm and in the absence of shocks to information quality (constant Aν) it follows a deterministic path toward a long-run steady state. This is due to the LLN which makes all the factors that affect beliefs to have predictable effects on dispersion. At the steady state these factors offset each other. The same holds true for the uncertainty, which means that with constant information quality (Aμ and Av are also constant) dispersion, uncertainty and speculation become constant once they reach their corresponding steady states.There are three factors that drive the dispersion ν˜: wealth reallocation, learning and information quality. Its law of motion has two terms. The first term shows how ν˜2 “decays”–the decay factor being φμ2κt2÷(1+ξt)–due to wealth reallocation and learning. The factor 1÷(1+ξt) represents the decrease in dispersion due to the reallocation of wealth and the factor κt2 represents the decrease in dispersion due to learning. The second term, σμ2Aν,t+1, does not allow the dispersion to vanish in the presence of disagreement (η>0). Every period, the dispersion receives a positive shock whose size depends on disagreement, since Aν increases with η, but is also limited by the information quality, since Aν decreases with ρ. For example, if the common signal is fully informative (ρ=1) then Aν equals zero.3.5. Large vs small economiesAn important implication of our model is that large economies, that exhibit a continuum of types, behave differently from small economies, populated by a small number of agent types. Arguably, the continuum of types is a more realistic representation of financial markets. However, theoretical studies commonly consider only a small number of types, typically two. There are two main differences in the equilibria of these economies.The first difference is that, in our large economy the equilibrium is stationary whereas in a small economy it is not. In our economy, the distribution across types αt(μ), which is characterized by μ˜t and ν˜t is stationary, which can be clearly seen from Proposition 3. The distribution across agents αti is not, but this is not relevant for the equilibrium in a large economy, that is, the equilibrium does not depend on the survival of individual agents.21 In a small economy, however, the equilibrium is not stationary, as shown by Yan (2008). He shows that in a model with a finite number of agents one agent survives and asset prices converge to the valuations of the surviving agent. Thus, the wealth distribution across beliefs is not stationary because the wealth distribution is not stationary. Only in the limit it is stationary, but it is trivially determined by the beliefs of the surviving agent, i.e., it becomes a homogeneous agent economy.The second and crucial distinction lies in the origin of fluctuations within the two economies. This disparity becomes evident when considering a constant level of disagreement (ζ). As mentioned in the preceding subsection, this condition leads to a constant market forecast dispersion and, consequently, constant speculative activity in the large economy. Conversely, in a small economy, the market forecast dispersion exhibits stochastic behavior and serves as the primary driver of excess stock price volatility. This finding is demonstrated by Dumas, Kurshev, and Uppal (2009) in their analysis of a model involving one rational agent and one overconfident agent.To illustrate both differences, consider our model economy with constant information quality (and disagreement) but only with two agents. Let αt be the consumption surplus share of the first agent. In this economy, the state that describes beliefs is given by (αt,μ˜t,ν˜t), whereas in the case of a continuum of agents, it is solely represented by μ˜t because ν˜ is constant. In the two agent economy, the market forecast dispersion is given byν˜t=2αt⋅(1−αt)⋅|ν¯t|. Both αt and ν¯t are stochastic, which means that ν˜t is stochastic even if ν¯t is constant. Furthermore, the significant fluctuations originate primarily from αt since it experiences almost permanent shocks, as evident from the following approximate law of motion22:αt+1≃αt+αt⋅(1−αt)γ[1−lt(gt+1)]. Here lt(gt+1) is the likelihood ratio of the second agent's beliefs relative to the first agent's beliefs. Therefore, the impact of speculation-induced shocks on the wealth distribution is crucial for ν˜t in a two-agent economy. However, in an economy with a continuum of types, these same shocks exhibit a deterministic effect on ν˜t, thanks to the LLN. Irrespective of the realization of gt+1, speculation consistently leads to a deterministic decrease in ν˜t. Moreover, this decrease is perfectly counterbalanced by the increase caused by disagreement at the steady state. Consequently, when dealing with a continuum of types, shocks to the wealth distribution do not have any asset pricing effects through the market forecast dispersion.Lastly, when considering the first difference, the non-stationarity becomes evident as αt will ultimately converge to the absorbing states of either 0 or 1. This implies that eventually one of the agents will be forced out of the market.
article.4. Qualitative analysis: The asset pricing properties of the model economy can be understood by the properties of three key quantities, the growth rate expectations, the price of risk, and the risk-free rate. Proposition 3 shows that disagreement affects the equilibrium through two channels, the market beliefs P˜ and the additional discount factor that depends on ξt. In this section we analyze qualitatively how these two channels affect the key asset pricing quantities. We start by specifying the habit process to provide explicit formulas for the risk-free rate and the price of risk.In our analysis, we assume that assets satisfy the fundamental asset pricing equation with the usual transversality condition to exclude asset price bubbles. That is, the price of an asset j with cash-flow stream (Dt+τj,τ>0) is given by the present value of its future cash-flows,Ptj=E˜t[M˜(zt,gt+1)(Pt+1j+Dt+1j)]=E˜t[∑τ=1∞M˜t,t+τDt+τj], where E˜ denotes the expectation under the probability measure P˜ and the stochastic discount factor (SDF) between periods t and t+τ is denoted by M˜t,t+τ=∏s=tτ−1M˜(zs,gs+1). Let also pdtj:=ln⁡(Ptj÷Dtj) denote the log price-to-dividend ratio of the asset j and note that the log return is given by rt+1j=ln⁡(1+epdt+1j)−pdtj+ln⁡(Dt+1j÷Dtj). We denote the log price-to-dividend ratio and return of the stock market with pd and rm, respectively.4.1. Habit specificationFollowing Campbell and Cochrane (1999) we specify the process of the log surplus consumption ratio st:=ln⁡[(Ct−Ht)÷Ct]. The log surplus consumption ratio follows the autoregressive process(16)st+1=φhst+(1−φh)s¯+λt⋅(gt+1−μ˜t), where s¯ is the unconditional mean and λt is the potentially time-varying conditional volatility. Campbell and Cochrane (1999) assume i.i.d. log consumption growth and, hence, instead of μ˜ they use the unconditional mean. The degree of risk aversion depends on how volatile the marginal utility is next period, where the marginal utility is given by (Ct+1−Ht+1)−γ=exp⁡[−γ⋅(st+1+ct+1)]. Hence, the degree of risk aversion depends on the conditional volatility of st+1+ct+1. Thus, it is (approximately) given by γ⋅(1+λt) and the price of risk is (approximately) given by the degree of risk aversion times the amount of risk, i.e. γ⋅(1+λt)σ˜t. These preferences nest the standard CRRA preferences when s¯=λt=0.For our analysis, it is convenient to define the zero mean state variable ωt:=(1−φh)(st−s¯), which follows the autoregressive process:(17)ωt+1=φhωt+(1−φh)λt⋅(gt+1−μ˜t). Note that as ϕ→1–when ϕ=1 s follows a random walk–the variance of ω goes to zero. To complete the characterization of the risk preferences, we need to specify λt. Following Campbell and Cochrane (1999), we choose λt so that the consumption surplus ratio (and ω) does not affect the risk-free rate. In general, variations in the consumption surplus ratio generate variations in the elasticity of intertemporal substitution, whereas fluctuations in the risk aversion generate fluctuations in the precautionary savings. Offsetting these two effects yields the specification for λ. To do so we consider a homogeneous agent economy, which is the case with no disagreement (ζ=η=0), where the stochastic discount factor (SDF) is given by the marginal rate of intertemporal substitution of the representative agent that consumes the per-capita consumption:(18)Nt,t+1=βe−γμ˜t+γωt−γ(1+λt)(gt+1−μ˜t). The risk-free rate is then given by the negative logarithm of the conditional expected SDF. The term of the risk-free rate that is related to habit is −γωt−0.5[γ(1+λt)σ˜t]2. To derive the specification of λt, we equate this term to its steady state value (i.e. when ωt=0) The steady state value of λ is given by θ and thus we obtain23(19)λt=(1+θ)2−2ωtγσ˜t2−1. In addition to β and γ, the preferences are thus determined by the habit persistence φh⩽1 and θ⩾0. When φh<1 the time-varying λ generates variations in the price of risk. To measure the effects of disagreement, we also consider a scenario where the price of risk is not influenced by ω. In this case, we set φh to 1, resulting in constant values for ω (equal to zero) and λ (equal to θ). A drawback of this specification is that the log surplus consumption ratio st follows a random walk, which means that it is unbounded. Nevertheless, we use it only as a tool to shut-down the time-varying risk aversion since, despite the shortcoming, prices are well defined. Setting θ=0 in addition to φh=1 we obtain the standard CRRA preferences.The following remark summarizes all equilibrium conditions. Remark 1Given the habit specification, the state of the economy is given by z=(μ˜,ν˜,v,χ,ω), which is driven by the shocks (g,sm,ϵχ,ϵρ). Proposition 3 together with the law of motion of v (Lemma 1) and the laws of motion of χ and ω, shown by equations (9), (10) and (17), fully characterize the dynamics of the state of the economy and the state prices that determine the prices of all financial assets.4.2. Some preliminariesBefore we analyze the three key asset pricing quantities, it is instructive to perform a comparative statics analysis of certain important variables that determine these key quantities, in terms of the risk aversion parameter γ and the information quality parameter ρ. The rest of the parameters take their estimated values, shown in Table 2.Fig. 1 shows how several quantities vary with γ and disagreement ζ, which ranges between η and 1. The parameter η is constant and equal to its estimated value, while information quality is given by ρ=η÷ζ. Perfect information and no disagreement corresponds to the case ζ=η (ρ=1), while maximum disagreement occurs when ζ=1 (ρ=η). Panel A shows the coefficients Aμ, Av and Aν, derived in Proposition 1. Naturally, Aν which determines the level of the market forecast dispersion ν˜ increases with ζ. The coefficient Aμ=ρ determines the sensitivity of the mean forecast to the common signal. Panel B shows the steady state values of v, ν¯ and ν˜ for two values of γ, 0.5 and 5.24 Clearly, the dispersion measures decrease with information quality. We also see that ν˜ increases with γ, which we formally prove below. Lemma 3Let ζ be constant and let (v,ν˜) correspond to the steady state values and let σ:=σc2+v2. Naturally, more risk averse agents (lower γ) speculate less and, thus, less wealth is reallocated to those agents whose beliefs are more correct in hindsight. Market expectationFluctuating growth rate expectations are a potential source of risk that can affect risk premia. For example, in the long-run risk literature, e.g. in Bansal and Yaron (2004), such fluctuations are the main source of risk. In our model these growth rate expectations are characterized by the market expectation μ˜. Here we analyze the role of disagreement, information quality, and their time variation regarding the properties of μ˜.Let us express again the process of μ˜, imposing the rationality constraint ζ=η÷ρ, for the case of constant information quality (and disagreement), in a slightly modified form.(20)μ˜t+1=φμμ˜t+(1−φμ)μc+σμρst+1m+φμ(1−κ1+ξ)(gt+1−μ˜t). We continue our comparative statics analysis to flesh out the qualitative effects. The volatility of the market expectation increases if, ceteris paribus, the coefficient of the growth rate shocks (gt+1−μ˜t) and/or the coefficient of the common signal st+1m increases. Regarding the equity premium, the SDF given by Proposition 3 and equation (18) implies that only the exposure to the growth rate shocks matters, while the common signal is not a priced risk.Let us start with the case of no disagreement (ξ=η=0). This takes us to Veronesi (2000), who analyzes the effect of information quality in a representative agent economy. As Veronesi (2000) shows, an increase in information quality ρ leads to a decrease in how sensitive μ˜ is to growth rate shocks, because the precision κ increases. If then the price-to-dividend ratio of the stock market reacts positively to expectation shocks, i.e., ∂pdt÷∂μ˜t>0 then an increase in information quality decreases the equity premium. In the opposite case (∂pdt÷∂μ˜t<0), which is the benchmark case in Veronesi (2000), the equity premium increases with information quality. However, the sign of ∂pdt÷∂μ˜t is determined by the relative strength of the usual cash-flow and discount rate effects.We now consider disagreement and remind that it is controlled by two parameters. The first is information quality that is controlled by ρ, where higher information quality leads to lower disagreement. The second is the perceived informativeness of the individual signals, which is controlled by the parameter η, where disagreement increases with η.We start with fixing information quality ρ and increasing η. The higher disagreement leads to higher speculation ξ and higher sensitivity of the market expectation μ˜t to growth rate shocks. Therefore, higher disagreement leads to higher volatility, as in Atmaz and Basak (2018). Further, the additional volatility also translates to a larger (smaller) equity premium if ∂pdt÷∂μ˜t>0 (<0).We then keep η constant and vary information quality ρ. Information quality affects the coefficients of both shocks to μ˜t and the effect on the volatility of μ˜t may be both positive and negative. First, higher information quality (ρ) implies higher exposure to the common signal sm, which could lead to higher volatility. However, this risk does not affect risk premia. Second, it leads to lower speculative activity (ξ). Third, it affects the precision κ that has a U-shape pattern in terms of information quality.25 As for the ratio κ÷(1+ξ), it may increase or decrease with information quality depending on the rest of the parameters. To examine the net effect on the volatility, we plot in Panel E of Fig. 1 the ratio of the unconditional volatility of μ˜ to the unconditional volatility of μ, against disagreement (ζ=η/ρ). We do so for three different values of γ, ranging from 0.5 to 5.0. We note first that the highest volatility is obtained with full information (ρ=1), regardless of γ. We then note that for γ=1.5 and γ=5.0, the volatility of μ˜ increases monotonically with ρ, while for γ=0.5, for low values of ζ the volatility decreases and then increases for higher values of ζ. In Panel F we plot the exposure to growth rate shocks 1−κ÷(1+ξ) and we note that for γ=0.5 it increases monotonically with disagreement. Otherwise, there is a U-shape pattern, which means that the effect on the equity premium can be both positive and negative from an increase in information quality.Therefore, it is not always true that an increase in disagreement due to lower information quality generates more volatile market expectation, where the opposite is typically true. This is contrary to most of the earlier literature on disagreement, including Atmaz and Basak (2018), that find that disagreement leads to higher volatility in expectations. The main difference lies in the introduction of the common signal, which is needed to explain the volatile mean forecast in the data, and the fact that disagreement is inversely related to information quality.4.4. The risk-free rateNext, we look at the one-period risk-free rate which is given by rtf=−ln⁡E˜t(M˜t+1).Lemma 4The continuously compounded risk-free rate is given byrtf=−ln⁡(β)+γμ˜t+γ−12ln⁡(1+ξt)−12γ2(1+θ)2σt2(1+ξt). We note first that by setting θ=0 we obtain the risk-free rate in the case of CRRA preferences. If we further assume no disagreement and perfect rationality, we obtain the standard expression for the risk-free rate in a representative agent economy with CRRA preferences, where μ˜t=μˇt.We then note that in the case of disagreement and constant information quality, both ξt and σt are constant. In this case, the risk-free rate is driven only by the market expectation μ˜t. When information quality is stochastic, the risk-free rate is also driven by the speculative activity ξt and σt.The third term in the equation of Lemma 4, represents the effect already discussed in the previous section, whereby forecast dispersion along with the speculative positions taken by the agents affect the discount rate, depending on whether γ is lower or higher than one. Specifically, with γ>1, the “pessimists” of each state have a stronger influence causing all state prices to drop and, as a result, the risk-free rate increases.The fourth term represents the precautionary savings motive, which becomes stronger with speculation and depends on two things: First, it depends on the steady state risk aversion γ⋅(1+θ), where 1+θ is the amplification generated by habit. Second, it depends on the representative agent consumption risk as given by σt1+ξt. When γ>1, the speculative activity has two opposite effects on the risk-free rate. For most parameters and for most states, the third term is larger in magnitude, which implies that when γ>1 the risk-free rate increases with the speculative activity ξt. In the opposite case (γ<1), an increase in speculative activity always pushes the risk-free rate down.Moving on to the quantitative analysis, in Panels A and B of Fig. 2, we plot the risk-free rate for three different values of γ: 0.5, 1.5 and 5. In Panel A, the value of θ depends on γ, so that the degree of risk aversion γ⋅(1+θ) is constant and equal to 25. We choose this value, because about this high a degree of risk aversion is needed to explain the market price of risk. These plots are useful to understand how these quantities vary across time when information quality and disagreement are stochastic. Panel A shows that the risk-free rate becomes more sensitive to disagreement as γ deviates from 1. The risk-free rate varies considerably even when γ is as low as 1.5, in which case it varies from around 0% to around 10%, reminding that the model is estimated at the quarterly frequency.Download: Download high-res image (436KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 2. The price of riskThe price of risk is the compensation that investors require for bearing a unit of systematic risk. However, since it involves an expectation, it is specific to the probability measure. We define it as follows: Let rg denote the log-return on the claim to the one-period per-capita consumption growth. Under a probability measure Q, the price of risk is defined according to(21)qtQ:=EtQ[rt+1g−rtf]+0.5VtQ(rt+1g)VtQ(rt+1), where EQ and VQ are the expectation and variance operators under Q. The definition of the price of risk is approximately the maximum Sharpe ratio.Agents, since they differ in their expectations regarding the growth rate of the economy, they also differ in the way they price risk and, in general, they differ in their expectations regarding returns of assets that bear systematic risk. More optimistic agents place a higher price on risk because their optimism leads them to take up more risk. This is clear from the following expression that shows the logarithmic growth in an agent's consumption surplus:log⁡(Ct+1i−Ht+1Cti−Ht)=μti−μ˜tγσt2(gt+1−μ˜t)−(gt+1−μ˜t)2+(μ˜t−μti)22γσt2+L(zt,gt+1). The expression derives from Proposition 2, where L(zt,gt+1) is agent independent. Therefore, an agent gains exposure to the aggregate risk depending on his level of optimism (μti−μ˜t)÷σt2, which also determines the agent's price of risk. Below, we provide the price of risk under the market beliefs and under the true probability measure.Lemma 5The price of risk under the market beliefs P˜ and under the true probability measure P is given byq˜t=γ⋅(1+λt)σ˜tandqt=q˜tσ˜tσc−μ˜t−μtσc−12σ˜t2−σc2σc, respectively.In the case of CRRA preferences we have λt=0. In a full information economy we have σ˜=σc and μ˜=μ, whereas for a perfectly rational homogeneous agent economy we have σ˜2=σc2+vˇ2 and μ˜=μˇ.We mostly care about q, the price of risk under the true probability measure, because it is the most relevant quantity for the time-series properties of prices. We first see that the effects of disagreement and information quality on q come through the market beliefs (μ˜,σ˜), and we note that they are independent of habit. The price of risk q has three terms: The third term comes from the variance correction between log and net returns.26 The second term depends on the bias of the market expectation relative to the true conditional mean. A positive bias (μ˜t>μ¯t) implies a lower price of risk and “overpriced” assets. This term implies that certain variations in excess returns come from the fluctuations in this bias, whose time-series process (given the rationality constraint ζt=η÷ρt) is given by(22)μ˜t+1−μt+1=φμ(μ˜t−μt)+σμ(ρt+1st+1m−ϵt+1μ)+φμ(1−κt1+ξt)(gt+1−μ˜t). Since Et(ϵt+1μ|st+1m)=0, this bias in the market expectation is on average zero. Further, it mean-reverts, which generates some mean-reversion in returns. We note that this mean reversion is not only a property of disagreement but also of learning. For example, in a model with no disagreement and perfect rationality, where ξt=0 and κt=κˇt, we would also have mean-reversion in the market bias.The first term of qt is the price of risk in the perfect information case, i.e. γ⋅(1+λt)σc, augmented by speculation and uncertainty, since σ˜t2=(σc2+vt2)(1+ξt). Hence, qt is first driven by the consumption level relative to the reference point (habit). When per-capita consumption is close to the reference point (lower ω), the price of risk is higher due to λt. We remind that in the case φh=1, λt is constant and equal to θ. Further, we have seen that the speculative activity is sensitive to the utility curvature parameter when this is low. This raises the question of whether for sufficiently low values of γ the speculative activity can help explain the high price of risk observed in the data through the term σ˜t/σc. We know that, for reasonable levels of risk aversion, CRRA preferences cannot explain the high price of risk. The lemma below shows that disagreement cannot remedy this. Lemma 6Let ζ be constant and ξ is the steady state value of the speculative activity. Also, let σ:=σc2+v2. Then, the following holds:∂qt∂γ=[−12∂ξ∂γ+(1+λt)(1+ξ+γ∂ξ∂γ)]σ2σc>0. Essentially, the price of risk is always lower when γ is lower, despite the higher speculative activity. Yet, in the presence of habit and if the time-step is not negligible, disagreement can have a significant effect on the price of risk if λ is large.In Panels C and D of Fig. 2 we can appreciate the quantitative effects on the price of risk. In Panel C we observe that all three parameterizations start with the same price of risk when information quality is at its maximum (ζ=η). We then see that the price of risk is much more sensitive to disagreement when γ is equal to 0.5, because with lower γ the speculative activity increases more with ζ. For γ=1.5 and γ=5, the price of risk is hump-shaped because the speculative activity is relatively weak, and the effect of uncertainty is stronger. In Panel D we observe what we noted with Lemma 6. First, that the price of risk increases with γ, independently of the level of disagreement and, second, that when θ (the steady state value of λ) is not particularly high, the price of risk is not very sensitive to changes in disagreement.
article.5. Quantitative analysis: Given the estimated model of disagreement, we can calibrate the preferences and study the quantitative implications in our model. We study the price properties of three assets; the per-capita consumption claim, the one-period risk-free rate and the claim to the stock market dividend. In the data, the stock market dividend process differs substantially from the per-capita consumption process, therefore, following Kaltenbrunner and Lochstoer (2010), we specify the following log dividend growth process:gt+1d=μc+βd⋅(gt+1−μc)+σdϵt+1d, where ϵd is iid. standard normal. The exposure to the per-capita log consumption growth is set to βd=2.15, to match the annual correlation between g and gd in the data (0.52), and the idiosyncratic volatility is set to σd=0.0575, to match the annual volatility of gd (11.10%).27The above specification of dividends does not exclude the possibility of the aggregate dividend outgrowing the aggregate consumption. However, we adopt it because the price-to-dividend ratio is stationary, it allows us to match the properties of dividend growth, and it facilitates computations because it does not add any state variables. A more appropriate approach to modelling stock market leverage is the one adopted by Barro (2006). However, this would constitute computations infeasible, because in this case the dividend growth gt+1d and the price-to-dividend ratio would depend on the state of the economy both in period t and t−1, that is, it would double the number of state variables.We approximate the price-to-dividend ratio and the price-to-consumption ratio of the dividend and consumption claims, respectively, using a projection method. The numerical method, despite having up to 5 state variables, yields very accurate results, judging by the Euler equation errors.5.1. Data and calibration of preferencesThe asset prices data is quarterly and covers the period from 1927Q1 to 2016Q4. As risk-free rate we select the 3-month nominal T-bill rate, obtained from CRSP, adjusted for quarter ahead expected inflation. Expected inflation is estimated using a fitted AR(1) process on the quarterly inflation series from the Bureau of Labor Statistics, where it is estimated as the change in the CPI index. For stock market return we use the CRSP value-weighted return, including dividend distributions. In addition, we compute the price-to-dividend ratio for the stock market using also the value-weighted returns excluding dividends.28 Because dividends are highly seasonal, we compute the price-to-dividend ratio as the price at the beginning of a quarter divided by the average quarterly aggregate dividend over the following year. For the same reason, we calibrate the model's dividend process to the annual dividend growth series in the data, by time-aggregating the quarterly series simulated with the model.To calibrate the model we need to determine four parameter values, shown in Table 4: (i) The subjective discount factor β is selected to match the average risk-free rate. (ii) The utility curvature parameter γ is chosen to match the volatility of the risk-free rate. (iii) The volatility of the log surplus consumption ratio at the average state θ allows us to match the stock market Sharpe ratio (SRm). (iv) The habit persistence φh is chosen to match the first-lag autocorrelation of the stock market log price-to-dividend ratio (pd).Table 3 shows statistics of asset prices, both from the data and from the model. Model 1 refers to the full model with both time-varying information quality and time-varying risk aversion. The parentheses next to the data estimates show standard errors. The parentheses next to the model(s) statistics show the t-statistics of the hypothesis that the data estimate is equal to the value given by the model. The model matches well the quantities that is calibrated to, namely the autocorrelation of pd with a t-statistic of 0.88, the mean and volatility of rf with t-statistics of 0.16 and 0.10, respectively, and SRm with a t-statistic of 0.11.Table 3. Additionally, it matches the autocorrelation of the risk-free rate, which is equal to 0.65, and generates a low correlation between the risk-free rate and the log price-to-dividend ratio, which is at the margin statistically different from the data estimate, with a t-statistic of 1.96.Regarding the pd-ratio, we see that the model generates fairly high volatility of 0.31, yet it is low compared to our data estimate of 0.44. Other studies, however, report lower data estimates that are similar to our model's prediction.29 Using similar data, Boudoukh et al. (2007) report a volatility of 0.41 excluding share repurchases and between 0.29 and 0.33 including share repurchases, depending on how the repurchases are measured. As for the average pd-ratio, for our model it is 4.34, which is a bit low compared to our data estimate of 4.71. Boudoukh et al. (2007) report estimates between 4.54 and 4.66.30 The low model average is mostly due to the fact that the average market return in the model (1.82%) is higher than in the data (1.59%). The higher average market return is the result of higher market return volatility generated by the model.The utility curvature parameter γ was calibrated to 1.17. With similar preferences, Campbell and Cochrane (1999) and Wachter (2006) use a value of 2.0. In our case, the value for this parameter is pinned down by the volatility of the risk-free rate. As we have already discussed, given the fluctuations in the speculative activity, a higher value for γ would imply a higher volatility for the risk-free rate, as it can also be inferred from Panel A of Fig. 2. Also, higher γ implies lower elasticity of inter-temporal substitution and, thus, larger fluctuations coming from changes in the market expectation μ˜. Our estimate of the risk-free rate volatility is conservative given that we adjust for the expected, rather than the realized, inflation. With a higher γ, the model can generate even higher volatility for the pd-ratio. For example, with γ=1.4 the model explains entirely our estimate of the volatility in the pd-ratio, but with twice as much volatility in the risk-free rate.Overall, the benchmark calibration captures very well the main moments of the stock market prices and returns, of the risk-free rate, and the correlation between them. In addition, the model explains the strong negative relation between the stock market and the dispersion in forecasts. In the data this correlation is −0.57 while the model generates a stronger negative correlation of −0.76. The difference is statistically significant, but the model is not calibrated to fit this moment.Finally, we also report moments of the log price-to-consumption ratio (pc) and the returns of the per-capita consumption claim (rc), however, we lack a data counterpart. The moments of the pc-ratio are comparable to those of the pd-ratio, though it is less volatile. The average returns of the consumption claim are very similar to the market returns in the data, but with lower volatility, due to the lower volatility in cash-flows. Despite this, the equity premium of the consumption claim is very close to the stock market equity premium in the data. Thus, the Sharpe ratio of the consumption claim is higher, close to 0.20, compared with the stock market Sharpe ratio of 0.14. This is due to the fact that the stock market in the model carries some idiosyncratic volatility, coming from the idiosyncratic dividend shocks.5.2. The stock market volatilityAn important implication of our disagreement model is that it generates significant excess volatility comparable to what we see in the data. It is well known that the Campbell and Cochrane (1999) habit-formation preferences, that we adopt, generate significant excess volatility. The question, therefore, is how much disagreement contributes to the stock market volatility that the model generates and in what way.We start answering this question by plotting in Fig. 3 the risk-free rate and the log price-to-dividend ratio against all the state variables. For each plot, we vary one state variable while we keep the rest at their steady-state values. The gray area in each plot shows the unconditional distribution of the corresponding state variable. The first thing we note is that the market expectation μ˜ (Panel A), the uncertainty v (Panel D), and the market forecast dispersion ν˜ (Panel C) do not generate significant stock price variation. The important stock price drivers are shown in Panels E and F, namely χ, that drives information quality, and ω, that drives risk aversion, respectively.
