The volatility of the stock market excess return in Model 3 is 6.54%, which is almost entirely generated by the exogenously specified dividend growth process. In Model 3, the only drivers of stock price variations are the market expectation μ˜ and the uncertainty v. It is clear that none of these variables are important drivers of stock prices. Uncertainty generates some small variations in the price of risk, and in addition it is not particularly persistent (see Table 6).Several studies, e.g., Veronesi, 1999, Veronesi, 2000, Brennan and Xia (2001), Atmaz and Basak (2018), and Martin and Papadimitriou (2022), focus on the implications of learning and/or heterogeneity in expectations and how they affect asset prices. The main property, that we already saw with equation (22) and the price of risk q in Lemma 5, is that disagreement and learning induce mean reversion in stock returns. However, we see that fluctuations in the market expectation hardly affect stock prices, which means that the mean-reversion effect is negligible. To understand why, we derive an approximate expression of the sensitivity of the pd-ratio to changes in the market expectation,(23)∂pdt∂μ˜t≈βd−γ1−φμZt, where Zt is the price-dividend ratio of the one-period dividend claim, which is very close to one, and at the steady state it is equal to 0.994. We see that a change in μ˜ involves the usual discount rate effect given by γ, the inverse of the EIS, and the cash-flow effect through βd. In our model the cash-flow effect is stronger, since βd=2.15 and γ=1.17. The value of the above sensitivity is 4.23, which together with the volatility of μ˜ (0.56%, shown in Table 6) gives a volatility for the pd-ratio of only 0.023. We compare this result with the model of Bansal and Yaron (2004) where fluctuations in growth expectations are important.32 The main difference is that they assume φμ to be equal to 0.979, which is much larger than our estimate of 0.773. In our model, the estimate of φμ largely reflects the persistency in the mean forecast of the professional forecasters in the data.Hence, the importance of disagreement regarding the stock price volatility goes through the time-variations in information quality. Going from Model 3 to Model 2, the pd-ratio volatility increases from 0.02 to 0.16 and the pc-ratio volatility from zero to 0.14 (Table 3). To confirm that it is disagreement that makes variations in information quality important for stock prices, in Model 6 we remove disagreement and keep the time variations in information quality. We see that the pd volatility drops from 0.16 in Model 2 to 0.03. This is not surprising since variations in information quality in the absence of disagreement affect the time-series properties of the market expectation and uncertainty, which we have already seen are not important for equities. It is clear, therefore, that it is not information quality itself that is important for equities but the fact that it drives disagreement (ζ).The stock price volatilities in Model 2 are a bit more than half of the volatilities in Model 1, where we also have time-varying risk aversion. In Table 6, we also see that ω, the driver of risk aversion, and χ, the driver of information quality and disagreement are strongly correlated, with a correlation of 0.90. This means that the movements in the price of risk generated by habit are very highly correlated with the movements in the risk-free rate generated by disagreement. Also, they have additive effects on stock prices, because increases in the equity premium due to habit are associated with increases in the risk-free rate due to disagreement, and both cause stock prices to decrease. This is an outcome of the fact that information quality is estimated to be mainly driven and positively affected by aggregate consumption shocks.Therefore, variations in information quality in the presence of disagreement are at least as, if not more, important for the stock price volatility. This is confirmed by Model 4 where we remove the variations in information quality and we see that the pd volatility drops from 0.31 in Model 1 to 0.14 (Table 5), which is slightly lower than the volatility we see in Model 2. Regarding the pc volatility we see that in Model 2 it is 0.14 whereas in Model 4 it is almost one-third at 0.05, which indicates that variations in information quality in the presence of disagreement generate more equity volatility than variations in risk aversion.We add time-variations in information quality to Model 4 and remove disagreement to obtain Model 5. We see that the volatility of pd drops slightly from 0.14 to 0.11, even though the risk-free rate becomes more volatile–0.61 in Model 4 to 0.79 in Model 5 (Table 5). The reason for these effects is the removal of disagreement, which makes the market expectation less sensitive to growth rate shocks but more volatile overall. Therefore, the higher volatility in μ˜ increases the risk-free rate volatility but the lower sensitivity to growth rate shocks decreases its correlation with ω. The drop in the correlation decreases the pd volatility. This also explains why the market Sharpe ratio drops slightly from 0.13 in Model 4 to 0.12 in Model 5.Comparing Model 5 with Model 1 is one more way to see the impact of disagreement in the presence of variations in information quality. Specifically, removing disagreement from Model 1 cuts the pd volatility to almost one-third, from 0.31 to 0.11 in Model 5. The change is even more dramatic for the consumption claim since the pc volatility goes from 0.25 in Model 1 to just 0.03 in Model 5.Closing, we want to clarify the role of the market forecast dispersion ν˜ and disagreement ζ. In Model 1, we see that ν˜ is strongly correlated with the stock market pd, with a correlation of −0.76. However, itself is not an important stock market driver, as it is evident from Panel C of Fig. 3, while it is very important for the interest rate. The strong correlation with the stock price is due to the fact that ν˜ is driven by information quality. In addition, information quality is highly correlated with ω. Consequently, ν˜ is highly correlated with the two important stock market drivers; it has correlations of −0.73 and −0.67 with χ and ω, respectively (Table 6). Finally, the reason why variations in information quality are so important in the presence of disagreement is because they bring about persistent shocks to the risk-free rate. An increase in information quality (ρ) decreases the amount of disagreement (ζ), which causes a decrease in the market forecast dispersion (ν˜) and, hence, speculation (ξ). The decrease in speculation lowers the risk-free rate. Information quality shocks are persistent and, thus, such movements translate into persistent changes in the discount rate and large changes in stock prices.5.4. Campbell-Shiller decompositionsDespite econometric challenges, the empirical evidence available regarding the source of fluctuations in the stock market pd-ratio, e.g., Shiller (1984), Campbell and Shiller (1988), Fama and French (1988), and Cochrane (2008), point to fluctuations in the equity premium. Therefore, our model in which persistent interest rate movements are responsible for about half of the stock price volatility may generate counterfactual predictions regarding the relations of the stock market pd and future interest rates, excess returns, and cash-flows. For this reason, we perform a Campbell and Shiller (1988) variance decomposition analysis of the pd-ratio for our models and compare the results with the data. We present the analysis in Appendix A.The analysis reveals that the model closely aligns with the data when considering samples of similar length. Notably, the majority of stock price fluctuations—91% in the model and 79% in the data—are attributed to future stock excess returns. Importantly, the difference between these percentages is statistically insignificant. However, a very long simulation confirms our earlier finding that only 63% of the fluctuations in the pd-ratio are due to variations in future excess returns within the model. This discrepancy primarily arises from the fact that, in shorter samples, the Campbell-Shiller decomposition mistakenly interprets most of the variations generated by the risk-free rate–specifically, the persistent variations coming from information quality–as originating from changes in excess returns. In Model 2, for example, despite the expected excess return playing a lesser role due to constant risk aversion, the decomposition of 100-year samples attributes, on average, 71% of the variations to the excess return.5.5. The equity premiumDisagreement and time-varying information quality also have important implications regarding the equity premium. The risk premium of an asset is given by the product of the market price of risk and the systematic risk of the asset (quantity of risk), that is, the risk that is correlated with the SDF. Since the only priced risk is the aggregate consumption growth, the conditional risk premium of an asset j can be approximately written as follows,Et(rt+1j−rtf)+12σt(rt+1j)≃qt⋅corrt(rt+1j,gt+1)⋅σt(rt+1j) where σt(⋅) and corrt(⋅,⋅) denote conditional volatility and conditional correlation, respectively. Our qualitative analysis has shown that disagreement affects somewhat the price of risk through σ˜. However, the price of risk is very similar across the models we consider and, therefore, the differences we see arise from the quantity of risk.33In Model 3, where information quality is constant, the equity premium is 0.54%, which is about one-third of what is observed in the data (1.44%) and is almost entirely generated by the exogenous dividend risk.34 This implies that disagreement in the absence of variations in information quality has no significant effect on the equity premium. This comes at no surprise given our earlier results regarding the effects on the price of risk and the equity volatility. Moving to Model 2, however, the equity premium is 1.25%, which implies that stochastic information quality more than doubles the equity premium compared to Model 3. This increase is due to the excess stock return volatility caused by shocks to information quality in the presence of disagreement. This excess volatility constitutes systematic risk because information quality and disagreement are mainly driven by per-capita consumption growth shocks. If this were not the case, that is, if information quality's variations were independent of g, the equity premium would not be affected. In the absence of disagreement, however, the introduction of time variations in information quality in Model 6 results in a decrease rather than an increase in the equity premium, as it drops to 0.36%.In Model 1 where, compared to Model 2, we also have variations in risk aversion due to habit, the equity premium increases further to 1.66%, since the excess volatility generated by shocks to risk aversion also constitutes systematic risk. Interestingly, the equity premium in Model 4, which features only variations in risk aversion, is 1.05%, which is a bit lower than the equity premium in Model 2 (1.25%), which features only variations in information quality.The main conclusion of the analysis is that disagreement affects the equity premium only in the presence of variations in disagreement, which, in our model, result from variations in information quality. This becomes clear if we compare Model 5 to Model 1, where the difference is that Model 5 has no disagreement. As a result, the equity premium in Model 5 is 0.92% whereas in Model 1 it is equal to 1.66%. The difference in the risk premium of the consumption claim is even more dramatic, since this is equal to 0.08% in Model 5 and equal to 1.38% in Model 1.
article.6. Bond market implications: A key insight of our analysis so far is that variations in disagreement (ζ), caused by shocks to information quality (ρ), introduce a persistent component in the risk-free interest rate. Naturally, this generates implications for bond prices and bond returns, which we examine in this section. Despite of the fact that our model is not built for such purpose and despite its relative parsimony, we obtain results that are helpful in furthering our understanding of the properties and historical evolution of the real yield curve.For obvious reasons, most of the literature regarding the term-structure of interest rates has focused on nominal bonds. However, more recently with the available data on inflation-indexed bonds, the literature is gradually placing more focus on the real yields–see for example, Abrahams et al. (2016), D'Amico et al. (2018), and Hsu et al. (2021). To avoid further complexity with modelling inflation that is needed to analyze nominal bonds, we study the model implications regarding real yields.We first analyze the properties of the short-term (one-period) rate and whether the forecast dispersion relates to future short-term rates as predicted by the model. We then investigate whether, through the lens of the model, we can explain historical movements in the real yield curve by variations in the forecast dispersion, and, finally, we compare the slope of the yield curve and the term premium predicted by the model to what we observe in the data.6.1. The short-term rateLemma 4 shows that the short-rate fluctuates over time due to variations in the market expectation μ˜, the speculative activity ξ, and the uncertainty v. The speculative activity is driven by both the market forecast dispersion ν˜ and the uncertainty. Fig. 3 shows that all three variables are important drivers where the market expectation seems to be the one that generates the largest variations. In Model 3, where information quality is constant and, hence, the only stochastic component is the market expectation, the volatility is 0.61 (Table 3). In Model 1, the stochastic information quality introduces variations in ν˜ and v and the short-term rate volatility increases to 0.85.35 The autocorrelation decreases from 0.76 in Model 3 to 0.65 in Model 1, which matches the autocorrelation in the data, due to the negative correlation of −0.24 (Table 6) between ξ and μ˜.Further, in Model 1, the forecast dispersion ν¯ has a correlation with the risk-free rate of 0.51 which, together with the negative correlation of −0.23 between ν¯ and μ˜ (Table 6), indicates that the components related to disagreement and uncertainty are at least as important as the market expectation for the variations in the short-term rate in the model. In the data, however, the correlation between the forecast dispersion and the 1-quarter risk-free rate is 0.26, which is statistically different from the model prediction. We need to emphasize, however, that the real risk-free rate is not observable in the data and is only estimated using the nominal 1-quarter risk-free rate and a time-series estimate of expected inflation, which makes it challenging to draw inferences regarding the short-term rate. Interestingly, the correlation between the nominal 1-quarter interest rate and the forecast dispersion is 0.51 just as in the model.In many asset pricing models, as for example in Wachter (2006), the real short-term rate is perfectly or very strongly correlated with the stock market pd-ratio. This is also true in our Model 3. However, in the data the correlation is statistically indifferent from zero and equal to 0.08. Our full model reduces this correlation to −0.20, still statistically different from the data estimate, but only marginally. The reason for this improvement is that the market expectation μ˜, that is important for the fluctuations in the risk-free rate, is not important for the stock market pd. Also, μ˜ is weakly correlated with ν˜ and ω (see Table 6) which are the significant drivers of the pd-ratio.6.2. Predicting future short-term ratesIn our model, the fact that information quality drives the speculative activity and is highly persistent, implies that the speculative activity and the forecast dispersion should be related to both contemporaneous and future short-term rates. We examine, therefore, whether the correlations of ξ and ν with future short-term rates in the data are as predicted by the model. Model-implied real yieldsIn the same way we did for ξ, we use the model to estimate the time-series of the rest of the state variables and then use them to generate model-implied time-series for real yields. The estimates of the state variables are based on the mean forecast and forecast dispersion of real per-capita GDP growth that we used for the estimation of the disagreement model. We set the mean forecast μ˜ equal to the data counterpart of μ¯, while for the market forecast dispersion ν˜, we adjust the mean and standard deviation of the data counterpart of ν¯ to match the mean and standard deviation of the model market forecast dispersion ν˜.37 We infer the rest of the state variables by using the regression modelxt=a0+a1μ˜t+a2ν˜t+a3ν˜t2+ux,t, where x∈(v,χ,ω), that we estimate using model simulations. For the uncertainty v we set a1=0. The regression R2's for the estimated model relations are 66%, 61%, and 53% for v, χ and ω, respectively. Thus, we generate time-series for the five state variables from 1968Q3 to the end of 2016.Using the time-series of μ˜, ξ, and v, we generate model-implied time-series of the short-term rate, one for each model, and compare them to the data. These securities do not offer a perfect estimate of the real yields and, as D'Amico et al. (2018) show, in the first few years of their existence they demanded significant liquidity premia. Yet, they contain important information against which to evaluate our model. The yields to maturity in the model are given by:yt(τ)=−1τE˜t[∏s=0τ−1M˜(zt+s,gt+s+1)] For longer maturities, the yields depend on all five state variables; χ contains information about future interest rates and ω determines the bond risk-premia and their evolution. We note that there is an additional source of error in comparing the model with the data, from the fact that we cannot infer the model-implied time-series of all state variables perfectly.In Panel B of Table 8 we compare the data and the model over the period from 1999Q1 to 2016Q4, for which TIPS data is available.39 We included data on 1-quarter and 2-, 5-, 10-, and 20-year maturities. The 1-quarter series corresponds to the same series as in Panel A, but for this data period. The rest of the data are based on TIPS prices.We discuss the means in the following subsection because they are related to bond risk premia and we focus here on results regarding the volatilities and correlations. Regarding volatilities, we note first that the real yields in the data are significantly more volatile than the model-implied yields. This is mainly due to a downward trend in the data over this period that the models cannot account for.40 D'Amico et al. (2018) attributes part of this trend to a gradual decrease in the liquidity premia demanded by these securities. Another contributing factor could be the monetary policy over this period, which is outside the scope of our paper.Model 3, with only fluctuations in the market expectation and constant risk premia, predicts particularly low volatility for the long yields. The variations in disagreement of Models 1 and 2 significantly increase these volatilities. The fluctuations in risk premia, which constitute the difference between Model 1 and Model 2, increase further the volatilities of the yields with maturities longer than 5 years.Next, we examine the correlation between the model-predicted yields and the data presented in the right section of Panel B. Here we focus on maturities of 2 years or longer. We observe negative correlations in Models 3 to 6, indicating that these models generate counterfactual fluctuations in these yields. Specifically, the correlations for Models 4 to 6 range from −0.19 to −0.37. Model 3 fares only slightly better, with correlations ranging from −0.06 to −0.23. However, Models 1 and 2, which incorporate stochastic information quality, showcase significant improvements. In fact, all correlations in these models surpass those of any other models, and we notice strongly positive correlations for all maturities beyond 5 years. For instance, the correlation between Model 1-predicted yields and the data is 0.38, 0.42, and 0.45 for maturities of 5, 10, and 20 years, respectively. Therefore, these correlations, along with the observed improvement from Models 3 to 6, lend support to our theory on the impact of disagreement on asset prices.6.4. Real bond premiaA central bond market fact is the upward sloping nominal yield curve, which implies that long maturity bonds are risky assets for which investors require compensation. However, Campbell (1986) points out, in a representative agent setting with CRRA preferences, that the positive autocorrelation in consumption growth implies a downward sloping real yield curve. Effectively, a positive shock leads to an increase in the short-term rate, because of the need to smooth consumption over time, and under such conditions long-term real bonds are a hedge and should command a negative risk premium. For this reason, many asset pricing theories explain the upward sloping nominal yield curve with positive inflation risk premia, while at the same time predicting downward sloping real yield curve.41 However, TIPS based evidence as early as Roll (2004) point to a positively sloped real yield curve. Hsu et al. (2021) provide further supporting evidence and employ a model with monetary policy, nominal rigidities, and external habit preferences to reconcile the evidence.Hsu et al. (2021) employ a similar mechanism to Wachter (2006) to generate positive real bond premia based on habit-formation preferences. Wachter (2006) utilizes a version of the Campbell and Cochrane (1999) preferences, as we do, but instead allows the short-term rate to respond to aggregate shocks. With the intertemporal substitution motive reacting more strongly to the precautionary savings motive, a positive shock drives down the short-term rate.42In our model, the stochastic disagreement generates naturally an upward sloping real yield curve. But before we see how, let us consider Model 3, where the market expectation is the only stochastic component. From equation (20) we know that μ˜ responds positively to aggregate shocks because of both learning and speculation, and this positive correlation is well supported by the data (Table 1). As a result, the real yield curve in Model 3 is downward sloping, which we can see from the left section of Panel B of Table 8. The same is true for Models 4 to 6, where the time-varying risk aversion or time-varying information quality in the absence of variations to disagreement do not change this prediction. For example, Model 4 with constant information quality and stochastic risk aversion predicts a 20-year to 1-quarter term-spread of −1.60% over the period 1999Q1–2016Q4, in contrast to the term-spread of 2.92% in the data. Consistent with Roll (2004) and Hsu et al. (2021), the data indicates a strong positive slope in the real yield curve.In Table 8, however, we see that as soon as we introduce variations in disagreement through shocks to information quality in Models 1 and 2, the yield curve becomes upward-sloping with about the same slope as the one we see in the data, although with different curvature. The 20-year (1-quarter) average yield predicted by Model 1 over this period is 2.25% (−1.18%) compared to 2.12% (−0.70%) in the data. The model generates the positive slope because information quality that reacts positively to growth rate shocks, drives down expected future short-term rates through lower disagreement and speculative activity.The above results show that the full model goes a long way in explaining both the level and fluctuations in the real yields we see in the data. The success of the model in this respect implies that it should also be able to generate realistic bond returns. We examine this by comparing unconditional bond return moments predicted by the model to data estimates. Table 9 shows the means (Panel A) and volatilities (Panel B) of bond excess returns in the data and for the six model configurations. We also include stock excess returns for comparison. Since data on real yields is only available from 1999 and onwards, which is a period of steady decline in interest rates, we expect the average bond returns in this sample to overestimate the population means. Indeed, in column D1 of Table 9 we see that bond returns were higher than stock returns over this period. For this reason, we also compute the average excess returns for the 10-year and 20-year nominal pure discount bonds, for which data is available since 1971 and 1981, respectively. These figures for the unconditional means of Model 1 are 0.61% and 1.01%, respectively. We can adjust the data estimates downwards by about 30% using as reference the average returns of the 10-year nominal bond estimated over the different periods.43 Doing so, gives estimates of around 0.9% and 1.1% for the 10- and 20-year real bond average excess returns, that come closer but still higher than the model unconditional means. We could further adjust these estimates downwards to account for the decrease in the TIPS specific liquidity premium found by D'Amico et al. (2018). Hence, given the available data we infer that the full model generates realistic real term premia, without any indication of being excessively high. In contrast, Models 3 to 6, that do not feature time-varying disagreement, predict negative bond risk premia and, in general, lower volatilities compared to the data.Therefore, the evidence we provide indicates that while fluctuating disagreement is important for stock prices, it is even more so for bond prices. The model offers a mechanism with predictions that are largely consistent with both stock and bond market facts. It's worth noting that the disagreement model is estimated independently of asset prices, while the four preference parameters were chosen to fit basic asset pricing quantities. Additionally, we would like to emphasize that reference-dependent preferences are necessary to generate both bond and stock risk premia, as CRRA preferences with realistic risk aversion parameters cannot achieve this.
article.7. Incomplete markets: The derivation of the equilibrium in closed form makes use of the standard assumption of complete markets. However, the assumption that agents can trade on their individual signals may be considered too strong. In this section, we analyze the equilibrium of an incomplete market where agents are not able to trade on their own signals, but they are able to trade on the aggregate shocks. For simplicity, we refer to this case as incomplete markets. In addition to providing tractability, it represents the realistic case wherein investors are able to hedge and speculate on the aggregate risks of the economy, but not on their own private information.With complete markets, we have determined that disagreement has a negligible impact on asset price volatilities when information quality remains constant. The primary objective of this section is to investigate whether this prediction, along with other asset pricing implications, holds true in the context of incomplete markets. The ensuing analysis reveals that there is no significant deviation from this prediction, and the equilibrium observed in complete markets remains a valid approximation for our model parameterization. In fact, when agents have log-utility preferences, the equilibria in the two cases are identical.The reason for this result is as follows: Incomplete markets modify the equilibrium to the extent that the optimal consumption-to-wealth ratio varies with an agent's expectation. However, as we observe with complete markets, the wealth to consumption ratio remains largely unaffected by expectations. This suggests that the quantitative effects of market incompleteness are minimal.Analyzing incomplete market equilibria is particularly challenging due to the fact that the state vector also includes endogenous variables, e.g., the wealth distribution, as Dumas and Lyasoff (2012) explain. Several numerical approaches have been proposed in the literature that make the task manageable in certain cases, although they are often restricted by the curse of dimensionality problem.44 As to whether market incompleteness matters, it depends on the type of incompleteness. For example, Gomes and Michaelides (2008) find that incomplete risk sharing, borrowing constraints, and life cycle considerations matter for the equity premium. More relevant for our paper is the study by Branger et al. (2020) who find that disagreement on jump risk is not material for asset prices.One other approach to analyzing incomplete market economies is through the use of fictitious completion proposed by Karatzas et al. (1991) to characterize the individual optimal policy. Gallmeyer and Hollifield (2008) utilizes this approach to analyze a disagreement model with two agents and portfolio constraints. In our case, we could adopt this approach by assuming that agents also have fictitious beliefs regarding their own signals so that the demand for these fictitious securities on their own signal would be zero. It turns out, however, that it would only introduce additional notation. We instead work with the actual optimality conditions and, with the use of the LLN on the infinite set of agents, we manage to characterize the equilibrium in terms of the consumption shares, similar to the case with complete markets.For simplicity and computational tractability we analyze the case of constant information quality, but the results generalize to the case with stochastic information quality. Here, we present the primary findings, while in Appendix B, we derive the equilibrium and provide a quantitative analysis.7.1. EquilibriumComplete markets enable us to solve for the equilibrium consumption processes without asset prices, using the equality between the SDF and the inter-temporal marginal rates of substitution state by state. In general, this is not possible with incomplete markets which requires solving for prices and policies simultaneously. Nevertheless, in our incomplete markets economy we can characterize the equilibrium in terms of the endogenous consumption allocations due to the LLN and show that the individual signals are expected to have insignificant effects in equilibrium.Agents would like to hedge their individual signals but they cannot. As a result, since their optimal consumption-to-wealth ratio is affected by their beliefs, it is also affected by their individual signals. Therefore, their consumption (surplus) shares, in addition to the way they speculate, are also determined by their individual signals. Nevertheless, due to the LLN, the individual signals do not enter the law of motion of the state of the economy.An essential quantity for characterizing the equilibrium is the conditional expectation Et[⋅|ϵt+1], which is over an individual signal, of the consumption (surplus) share:α¯(ϵt+1,αti,μti,zt):=Et[αt+1i|ϵt+1]. Here ϵt+1 refers to the set of aggregate shocks. The importance of α¯ is that in equilibrium it integrates to one because, by the LLN, α¯ is also the consumption share of the specific type. Consequently, the state of the economy zt remains defined by ωt and the consumption share distribution across types. With CRRA preferences, α¯ is independent of αi and, thus, agent types are still only determined by their beliefs. However, unlike with complete markets, the distribution of types is no longer Gaussian.When agents have habit preferences, α¯ also depends on αti because it influences the consumption-to-wealth ratio Qti. This occurs because, with habit preferences, wealth affects an individual's speculative activity. Agents whose consumption levels are close to their habit levels cannot engage in significant speculation. Thus, with habit preferences, the relevant state variable is αt(αti,μti) since agent types are determined by both wealth and beliefs. The proposition below characterizes the equilibrium.Proposition 5In the incomplete markets equilibrium the price of state zt+1 in period t is given by,pt(zt+1)=β(Ct+1−Ht+1Ct−Ht)−γ⋅[∫01αti[fti(ϵt+1)hti(ϵt+1)]1/γdi]γ where we define,hti(ϵt+1):=Et[(αt+1iα¯t+1i)−γ|ϵt+1]. The consumption surplus shares evolve according to,α¯t+1iαti=αti[fti(ϵt+1)hti(ϵt+1)]1/γ∫01αtj[ftj(ϵt+1)htj(ϵt+1)]1/γdj andαt+1iα¯t+1i=1/Qt+1iEt[1/Qt+1i|ϵt+1](1+e−st+1−1α¯t+1i)−e−st+1−1α¯t+1i, where Qi is characterized by equations (B.4) and (B.5).Proposition 5 demonstrates that the inability to trade on the individual signals affects the equilibrium to the extent that the wealth to consumption ratio Qt+1i varies with the individual signal, otherwise, if Qt+1i=Et(Qt+1i|ϵt+1) then αt+1i=α¯t+1i and the equilibrium is as in Proposition 2. Further, the effects on the equilibrium and asset prices come from two sources, the first being how state prices are determined given a distribution of types and the second the distribution of types and its law of motion.7.2. Log-utility preferencesIn the special case where agents exhibit log-utility preferences without habit, the market incompleteness does not affect the equilibrium because the agents do not need to trade on the individual signals.Corollary 2If agents exhibit log utility preferences (γ=1) and no habit (Ht=0) then the incomplete markets equilibrium is identical to the equilibrium with complete markets.This is the well-known case in which agents have no hedging demand because their consumption-to-wealth ratio is constant (Qti=1/(1−β)), irrespective of (their perception of) the investment opportunity set. Thus, from Proposition 5 we immediately see that hti=αt+1i/α¯t+1i=1 and that the law of motion of αi and the state prices become identical to those with complete markets.7.3. The general caseIn the more general case of power utility preferences with or without habit, the market incompleteness affects state prices if hi is on average different from one and/or it correlates with agent types. However, under a first-order approximation neither of these conditions apply since,hti(ϵt+1)≃Et[1−γαt+1i−α¯t+1iα¯t+1i|ϵt+1]=1. Therefore, given the same distribution of types in some period, state prices should be approximately the same as those with complete markets.In regards to the distribution of types and its law of motion, we also expect the market incompleteness to have minimal effect. Under the first-order approximation when hi=1, the first step in the change of αi over a certain period, that is, α¯t+1i/αti, is identical to the change in αi under complete markets. With incomplete markets, there is an additional change due to the individual signal, i.e., αt+1i/α¯t+1i. Nevertheless, this second step has no effect in the distribution of types when agents exhibit CRRA preferences and it is expected to have an insignificant effect in the general case with habit. In the case of CRRA preferences, the types are determined only by beliefs and this second step only redistributes wealth among agents who in the previous period had the same beliefs. Their total consumption share is unaffected and it is given by α¯t+1i.By the same argument, in the case with habit, the second step only affects the distribution of types αt(α,μ) across the dimension of α but not μ. However, the dimension of α only matters to the extent that the distribution of hi affects the state prices but we expect state prices to be insignificantly affected. In other words, if hi≃1 only the distribution across beliefs is important which is not affected by the second step. Finally, even if the distribution across α matters, the second step is entirely deterministic and, thus, does not add any stochastic effects to the law of motion of the distribution of types nor to asset prices.Hence, the importance of market incompleteness on asset prices depends on whether hi significantly differs from one. However, the quantitative analysis carried out in Appendix B based on the complete market equilibrium illustrates the opposite, that hi is expected to be very close to one in almost all states. Hence, the market incompleteness does not appear to alter significantly any of the effects we analyze.
article.8. Conclusions: In this study, we develop and solve a model of disagreement in a large economy populated by a continuum of agents with habit-forming preferences. These agents interpret information differently, and the extent of their disagreement is influenced by the quality of information. Our model is both stationary and elaborate, allowing us to estimate it using data on professional forecasts.Using this disagreement model, we derive a range of qualitative and quantitative results regarding asset prices. We find that when the quality of information remains constant, asset prices behave similarly to an economy without disagreement. This contradicts earlier research suggesting that disagreement leads to excess stock market volatility. Increased volatility in sentiment (market expectation) is one way in which disagreement can impact asset prices. It arises from the fact that speculative actions result in wealth redistribution towards individuals who are ultimately proven to be more accurate in hindsight. However, we observe minimal impact on asset prices. The second way in which prices are influenced is through variations in market forecast dispersion. However, unlike in the case with a finite number of agent types, with a continuum of agents the forecast dispersion is constant, unless the factor of disagreement fluctuates.The estimation of the model, however, reveals that information quality is volatile, persistent, and is primarily influenced by aggregate shocks. Positive shocks enhance information quality while reducing disagreement. This has several important stock and bond market implications that are supported by evidence. For example, it helps explain the equity premium and the stock market volatility, the correlation between the stock market and dispersion in forecasts, the limited correlation between the stock market and the risk-free rate, the upward sloping real yield curve, and variations in long-term real yields.We want to emphasize that, aside from the disagreement model that is estimated independently from asset pricing data, our model maintains parsimony by relying solely on the aggregate endowment shock to drive the economy's dynamics. Overall, our findings indicate that disagreement holds significant relevance as a fundamental asset pricing factor, although not through the channels conventionally explored in the literature. Instead, it operates through the factor(s) that underlie disagreement. In our model, this factor is information quality, which is both reasonable and supported by theoretical justifications.
